title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Public Works Committee approved several items including a resolution supporting state transportation funding, awarded a recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel for $126,900, and directed staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with fines of $100 per day. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and construction services for two road projects.

agenda.item: Approval of April 13, 2026 Public Works Committee Meeting Minutes
Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permits
2025 Weston Water Utility Public Service Commission Annual Reports

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a local road improvement grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project that will likely move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the approximately $1.5 million project. Michael noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion also covered Weston Avenue repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for road depression issues over the sanitary sewer trench, and upcoming repairs on Schofield Avenue and Fuller Street. A Bladel special assessment meeting was tentatively scheduled for Thursday, May 28th at 6:00 p.m.

Michael reported receiving updated modeling from Strand showing the pond will remove more phosphorus for Weston but less for Rothschild. An amendment to the inter-municipal agreement may be needed based on revised effectiveness calculations. Staff is attempting to get DOT to pay for their portion (approximately one quarter) which previously impacted grant eligibility.

Staff reported on spring activities including road limit removal on April 29th, mailbox and sign repairs, dewatering operations on Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week, curb line sweeping, hot mix patching, and storm damage cleanup. Jones Street culvert repairs were completed, adding a third 18-inch culvert. Vehicle maintenance included removing plow equipment, applying fluid film rust protection, and servicing mowing equipment. A new MAC tandem axle chassis has been ordered with upfitter items.

Josh reported on a service repair at Caribou Coffee, well maintenance items, and continued meter card maintenance. Sanitary side experienced high pump activity due to clear water infiltration. Data showed January had 36 million gallons pumped into the system with 32 million treated, but April showed 37 million in and 53-54 million treated - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. Staff discussed upcoming INI study with inpipe technologies to identify infiltration sources.

Michael presented the annual report showing operating net income of approximately $482,911, consistent with the prior year's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. The utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. The utility serves approximately 6,100 customers total: 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild, and 5 in Schofield/Rib Mountain. Cash position is $6.8 million. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael explained this League of Wisconsin Municipalities resolution supports sustainable state transportation funding. He noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and Wisconsin is generally affordable compared to neighboring states for automobile ownership. Motion to adopt Resolution 2026-014 passed unanimously.

Michael explained the Ryan Street site stockpiles concrete and asphalt that gets crushed into base course. King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton (versus $4.25 from another bidder) with estimated 30,000 tons totaling $126,900. Analysis showed village cost of $17.11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau paying $28/cubic yard, resulting in approximately $130,000 savings across three projects this year. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Discussion centered on enforcing existing code against sump pump discharges into sanitary sewers. Josh described finding clear water in lift stations, including one Kathleen station with only 5-6 houses where a sump pump was detected. Committee discussed whether violations are knowing or unknowing. Staff reported March flows were 43 million gallons versus 35.5 million the prior year, costing approximately $30,000 extra in March and $42,000 more in May. Committee directed staff to create enforcement language with fines of $100 per day. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael explained a vacant lot needs water and sewer service but mains don't currently extend there. Proposed temporary long lateral service until Radkey Avenue reconstruction, at which point the owner would pay assessment for main extension. Committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion passed unanimously.

Michael presented an intergovernmental agreement with Rothschild for Military Road construction. Weston's share is $94,860 for sanitary sewer ($6,156) and water main ($88,704) relocation due to storm sewer conflicts. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to approve agreement passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Construction Services for Alderson/Jelinek and Bladel Projects
discussions.body: Michael sought construction inspection services due to vacant engineering tech position. For Alderson/Jelinek roundabout: MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910; staff recommended MSA. For Bladel Avenue: MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730; staff recommended Clark Dietz due to having experienced full-time staff rather than a summer intern. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: Staff to draft enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines
Staff to modify Radkey Avenue agreement to include 2029 deadline for reconstruction
Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing to King Gravel at $4.23/ton ($126,900 estimated)
Recommend village board adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Recommend village board approve Military Road agreement with Rothschild for $94,860
Award Alderson/Jelinek construction services to MSA ($37,315)
Award Bladel Avenue construction services to Clark Dietz ($73,730)
Special assessment meeting scheduled for Bladel Avenue on May 28, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday
Staff to provide update on street lighting and water main replacement topics at future meeting

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially boosting attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Grant request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated for chair and elected unanimously. Cushing was nominated for vice chair by a member, seconded by Jackson, and elected unanimously. Renee was nominated and elected as secretary unanimously.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved. Motion was seconded by Renee and passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill (3308 Green Pastures Lane) represented the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield hot air balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making their event the sole balloon festival in the area. Commission members noted the application showed 20 fewer hotel rooms than last year and requested better tracking of hotel room usage for future applications. The commission approved a $5,000 grant matching last year's amount, with a motion passed unanimously. Commission requested the applicant provide hotel room data in their post-event report.

Renee reported on attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, noting it was the first time the event was hosted in Marathon County in decades. She described meeting the candidates, including winner Anastasia, and noted the candidates toured various Marathon County locations including Mullen's Cheese.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant to Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow Balloonfest requested to track and report hotel room usage data in post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection areas. Both items passed unanimously with conditions.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Reordering of agenda to take item six first
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey maps and site plans
Planning and development department monthly project update report
Future agenda items and staff updates

discussions.item: Site plan review - Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to face east due to a 36-inch gas pipeline running through the property, which significantly reduced fill costs. The building meets all B2 zoning requirements. Discussion included future expansion capability (possible to the north), placement of an alternate storage shed, and landscaping compliance. A motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Staff proposed a two-inspection-per-year program for salt storage facilities in wellhead protection areas - one warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and one winter operational inspection. The program uses a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT standards. Staff noted one existing facility near Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and commission agreed staff should reach out to bring them into compliance. Motion to approve the program with reporting back to both Plan Commission and Public Works passed unanimously.
Motion to approve minutes as presented passed unanimously with no discussion.

Staff announced the first joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee meeting for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. Discussion included potential future residential TIF districts for workforce housing, with staff noting Ehlers has prepared a presentation on qualifications that could be shared with commissioners.

publicComment: Jim Pensel of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via Zoom. He urged the commission to exercise common sense regarding landscaping requirements for the police department project, citing concerns about taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested the commission require annual reporting on the salt shed inspection program to ensure compliance visits are documented.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with conditions 2-5
Salt storage shed inspection program adopted with twice-yearly inspections and annual reporting to Plan Commission and Public Works
Staff to reach out to salt storage facility near Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Staff to send email and calendar invite for June 1st steering committee meeting for comprehensive plan
Electric vehicle charging stations added to future agenda (likely fall timeframe)
Next regular meeting scheduled for Monday, June 8, 2026 at 6:00 p.m.

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an orientation meeting for new and returning members, featuring educational presentations from four county departments: Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. The committee approved April 1st meeting minutes unanimously and scheduled North Central Healthcare for a separate future presentation.

agenda.item: Call to order and verification of remote attendance
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Discussion of operational functions required by statute
Review of standing committee ordinances, missions, duties and responsibilities

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavelli made a motion to approve the minutes. Vice Chair Rosenberg seconded. The motion passed unanimously with all members voting aye.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented on the department's mission to prevent disease, promote health, and protect against environmental hazards. She highlighted the department's 37.6 FTE staff across four divisions, their 1,200+ licenses for restaurants and public facilities, communicable disease prevention, nurse family partnership program, and new homeless system response position. The department has public health vending machines stocked with free resources including tick kits. Regarding pandemic preparedness, Suginaka stated the department has trained extensively since COVID and conducts regular measles response exercises.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa who could not attend. The department has nearly 140 FTE staff with a 2026 budget of over $22 million (roughly $7 million tax levy). Leonard highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program, developed in partnership with the board, which addresses housing instability as a root cause affecting child welfare and youth justice cases. He noted high-cost placements can reach $600,000 annually per child.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported his small team of 3.0 FTE had 8,149 interactions in 2025, up significantly from prior years. He noted $44 million in annual VA compensation flows to Marathon County veterans, with $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025 alone. Galindo highlighted suicide prevention efforts, noting 7 of the county's 30 suicides in 2025 were veterans. He described partnership with Center for Veterans Issues for housing vouchers serving 30-70 veterans. When asked about staffing adequacy, Galindo acknowledged the office is busy but performing well, having reduced appointment wait times from 6 weeks to under 3 weeks.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county ADRC serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties with approximately 60 FTE and 400 volunteers. He highlighted significant financial challenges with nutrition program costs up 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat. The Older Americans Act hasn't been reauthorized in two years. Ray noted Wisconsin leads the nation in falls and falls-related deaths, and described their Stepping On falls prevention program.

Administrator Leonard noted that North Central Healthcare Executive Director Jason Hake will be scheduled for a separate dedicated presentation before the committee due to the organization's complexity and budget integration requirements.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved unanimously
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate presentation
Chair Boots will bring strategic plan information to upcoming meetings
Committee members invited to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with topics for future agendas
Next meeting corrected to Tuesday June 3rd at 3:00 PM (not May 29th as initially stated)

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees, passed several ordinance amendments including mobile food vendor licensing and e-bike regulations, and held extensive discussion about the farmers market relocation to Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and approved various license renewals for the 2026-2027 term.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order, pledge of allegiance, roll call
Public hearing on ordinance 26-010 and resolution 2026-015 amending park and recreation impact fees
Approval of minutes from previous meetings
Acknowledge reports, minutes from boards, committees, and commissions
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Unfinished business - remote meeting attendance policy amendments
New business - appointments to committees and commissions
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Park and Recreation Impact Fee Amendment
discussions.body: Staff presented proposed increases to park and recreation impact fees from $300 per single family unit to $761, with proportional increases for duplex and multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel commented that the village lacks real data on park maintenance costs. Trustee Barb expressed support, stating the fee is justified as population growth increases park needs. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lope Sorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance creates a regional cooperative inspection program where Safer, Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and other municipalities will recognize each other's fire inspections for food trucks. Once inspected by any participating department, a sticker is placed on the truck eliminating duplicate inspections in each community. Ordinance 26-008 passed unanimously with motion by Lopes, second by Zagami.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting policy. Trustees agreed to strike sentences requiring specific justifications for remote attendance (health, safety, welfare) and language stating remote attendance is not authorized for vacation or convenience. The final policy requires only 12-hour advance notice to the administrator, clerk, and president; camera must be on; and remote attendance is not permitted during closed session. Amendment motion by Beck, second by Clark, passed with Hermling and Barb voting no. Final amended policy passed with the same two dissenting votes.

President appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopes, approved unanimously. The board agreed to include applicant resumes in future packets for transparency. Parks, HR/Finance, and CLIPS still have openings.

Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street requested secondhand article and jewelry dealer licenses. Chief Hunt explained this involves buying and selling precious metals with large dollar transactions, requiring tracking for potential stolen merchandise. The business location is still under construction, so the board amended the motion to require satisfactory background check completion and receipt of occupancy permit. Amendment passed unanimously, final motion passed unanimously.

Board held discussion-only on the farmers market relocation from Camp Phillips/Scoffield (private property being developed) to Kennedy Park. Trustee Beck raised safety concerns about parking on grass along Alderson Street. Renee explained market history: started with 25 stalls at old location, expanded to 42-53 vendors. Trustees debated Mont Miller Park as alternative but noted previous concerns about it being too far from traffic. Beck questioned finances but was assured the market operates at a gain. No action taken; discussion only.

Public Works Director Michael explained the village is seeking an engineering technician but noted the specific skill set is difficult to find locally. Two outside engineering contracts for project inspection are on the agenda for projects starting in June. He noted if an engineering tech is hired, outside firm contracts could be reduced. The village continues warranty work on Weston Avenue, Fuller, and Scoffield Avenue.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52 Street offered public comment on two occasions. First, he criticized the board for hiring outside engineering consultants at $108,000 when an engineering technician position remains unfilled, questioned why committee appointment discussions happen outside public meetings, and complained about lack of detailed Kennedy Park funding information in meeting packets. During the impact fee hearing, he argued the village lacks real data on park maintenance costs and is 'flying by the seat of our pants' with the fee increase.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased per ordinance 26-010 and resolution 2026-015
Mobile food vendor license ordinance 26-008 approved creating regional inspection cooperation
Ordinance 26-009 amending standing committees approved
Nuisances ordinance 26-011 approved
E-bike ordinance 26-012 approved
Resolution 2026-014 supporting long-term transportation funding approved
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove specific justification requirements
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC secondhand dealer license approved contingent on background check and occupancy permit
Compliance assurance plan approved
All license renewals for 2026-2027 term approved
Vouchers 66598-66740 and 90248-90251 approved
Staff directed to include committee applicant resumes in future meeting packets
Comprehensive plan steering committee first meeting scheduled for Monday, June 1 at 6:00 PM as joint meeting with plan commission
Weston Avenue speed limit study to come back to board after construction completion

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board of Supervisors held an adjourned organizational meeting where they approved committee appointments, elected Supervisors Hapa and Arstead to the executive committee at-large positions, and unanimously approved a resolution reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris from recent severe weather that impacted Ringle and other communities. The board suspended its rules to consider the emergency tipping fee resolution directly from the Solid Waste Management Board.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and moment of reflection
Committee appointments discussion and remarks from Chair
Election of at-large executive committee members
Resolution 24-26: Out of home placement reserve increase

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs delivered remarks thanking staff and Vice Chair Boots for assistance in developing committee appointments. He emphasized goals including increasing diversity of perspectives, ensuring supervisors can grow as leaders, and noting that over 34% of the board is in its first or second term. The committee appointments were approved by motion from Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of At-Large Executive Committee Members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated for two at-large positions: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg spoke in support of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's smallest children and oldest elders. Paper ballots were distributed and counted. Supervisors Hapa and Arstead were elected to the executive committee.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent by motion from Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Resolution 24-26: Out of Home Placement Reserve Increase
discussions.body: Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but the vote was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The resolution was approved but the vote was not unanimous.

Administrator Leonard and Director Dave Hagen Busher presented the resolution from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for disaster-related debris from recent severe weather. The standard $65/ton rate would be reduced to approximately $36.94/ton after state ($13.10) and town of Ringle ($2.33) fee waivers, plus the county's reduction. Two residents, Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck, were granted special permission to speak, requesting the county waive fees entirely for affected homeowners. Supervisor Rosenberg requested the public comment exception. The board first voted unanimously to suspend rules to consider the resolution directly. The resolution then passed unanimously by motion from Supervisor Arstead, seconded by Supervisor Morash.

publicComment: Two members of the public were granted special permission to speak on the tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty requested Marathon County waive its landfill fees entirely for disaster victims, noting the state, DNR, and town of Ringle are all waiving their portions, and requesting a six-month timeframe. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of homeowners whose houses must be demolished, noting many are underinsured and insurance adjusters haven't yet visited approximately 44 affected homes, requesting extended relief time.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisors Hapa and Arstead elected to at-large executive committee positions
Resolution 24-26 approved for out of home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment
Resolution 26-26 approved reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster-related debris through June 17th
Administrator Leonard to work with Community Foundation on mechanism for reimbursing unreimbursed insurance costs for disaster victims
Solid Waste Management Board to develop clear policy within six months on handling similar disaster situations in the future
County officials workshops available - supervisors encouraged to contact clerk

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to forward a modified fee waiver resolution to the county board to help property owners impacted by the April 17, 2026 tornado. The committee amended the fee waiver to only cover septic system reconnect fees ($160), rejecting broader fee waivers for other zoning and septic permits.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan)
Consideration of fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacts
Department overview: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry
Department overview: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes with Supervisor Drebeck seconding. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

Garrett Pogle presented the staff report for rezoning from urban residential and general agriculture to rural residential in the Town of Green Valley. The Town Board recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered for or against. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and town approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (McMillan)
discussions.body: The petition sought to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture in the Town of McMillan. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy designation, and staff explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, with the rezoning needed to make the home a conforming use. Staff confirmed no natural areas would be lost. The Town Board recommended approval April 20, 2026. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waiver consideration for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacts
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties impacted by the tornado. Significant debate ensued. Supervisor Drew questioned the precedent and fairness to others not in declared disaster areas. Supervisor Drebeck expressed concern about costs and questioned whether the county was truly financially constrained given recent fee increases. Marilyn Bend asked about mound system costs. Eric Vogel questioned whether homeowners insurance would cover fees. Supervisor Drebeck moved to amend the resolution to only waive reconnect fees ($160), not other zoning or septic permit fees. The amendment was seconded by Supervisor K and passed. The amended resolution then passed on a non-unanimous vote to forward to the county board.

Director David Hagenbusher presented an educational overview covering the department's 16-year history, landfill operations, the renewable natural gas partnership with Vidi Energy launched in 2023, and upcoming capital projects including an $6 million cell expansion and leachate treatment facility required by 2032 under new state law. He noted lithium-ion battery fires occur about once weekly at the facility.

Tom Lovelin, County Forest Administrator, presented on behalf of Director Jamie who had an emergency. He outlined the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal staff serving both county and city systems. He noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Tom Lovelin is retiring June 1st after nearly 30 years of service, with Joe Tucker selected as his replacement.

actionItems: Rezoning for James Offer and David Tract (Green Valley) approved and forwarded to county board
Rezoning for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz (McMillan) approved and forwarded to county board
Modified fee waiver resolution (reconnect fees only) forwarded to county board for April 17, 2026 tornado-impacted properties
CPZ staff to track costs and requests related to fee waivers for future policy consideration
Conservation services educational presentation planned for summer meeting

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee approved kayak launch signs for the Plover River and deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center until next month when the absent chair can participate. The committee also received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising progress and discussed graffiti removal challenges on the Oaklair River Trail.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog day at the aquatic center discussion
Graffiti on Oaklair River Trail discussion
Remarks from staff and committee members

discussions.body: Minutes from April 27, 2026 meeting were approved unanimously with a motion and second. No discussion was held.

Director Sean Oster provided a timeline update on the Kennedy Park master plan project, noting the CORP was approved in November 2023 and the master plan the following month. Scott asked clarifying questions about the 10-year fundraising timeline and village capital funds. Oster explained the Friends of Kennedy Park are currently fundraising for $117,000 for upper field lights and have approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation. He noted Youth Baseball contributed $8,700 for equipment and the friends group plans a public check presentation at their June tournament. Committee members emphasized this is a long-range volunteer effort and the master plan serves as a guide that can be adjusted over time.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Staff provided photos and maps showing the approximate location of trees removed from the Plover River and the installation of wood mats over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. Dan explained the mats were installed by backing equipment onto them, with additional material added for approaches. TC Energy (formerly ATC/ANR Pipeline) was on site to monitor the work.

The item was deferred to next month because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A member of the public, Andy (6504 [address inaudible]), spoke against allowing dogs in the public pool, stating 'There should not be animals allowed into a public pool. Period.' The chair noted Andy's objection would be recorded and he was welcome to return next month.

Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and memorial benches on the trail, noting the pressure washer cannot access the area and benches weigh 2,000 pounds each. Options discussed included anti-vandalism coatings, luster seal, and volunteer work days. Committee member suggested community volunteers or groups like Boy Scouts could help with annual maintenance. No formal action was taken; staff will investigate cleaning options.

Dan Stroming from Marathon County Parks presented version seven of proposed signs for Plover River kayak launches. Signs will be installed at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks in Weston, plus locations in Schofield. The signs include maps, river gauge QR codes, and location-specific historical information. Marathon County Parks Foundation funded the signs through state grants. Motion to approve the signs and forward to village board passed unanimously.

Staff requested discussion on food allergy awareness signage for playgrounds after receiving an email from an individual offering to provide signs encouraging people to keep food at picnic tables rather than play structures. Committee agreed to bring the item back for future discussion.

publicComment: Two members of the public commented. Andy (6504 [address inaudible]) spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating dogs belong in rivers and lakes, not public pools. Jim Pensan (5002 [Street] Weston), speaking online under the name 'Vern M,' criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned the Kennedy Park funding report, calling the $8,700 expenditure figure 'too coincidental' and asking where the $10,000 grant money, glow event proceeds, and lighting funds are, stating the 'numbers presented are extremely convoluted and misleading.'
actionItems: Approve installation of Plover River kayak launch signs at Yellow Banks, Ross Avenue, and Highway J locations - forward to village board
Bring dog day at aquatic center discussion back next month when Chair Katrina is present
Staff to investigate graffiti removal and cleaning options for Oaklair River Trail
Add food allergy awareness signage discussion to future agenda
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house June 11th, 5-7 p.m. in training room

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee approved previous meeting minutes and acknowledged April financial reports including budget status and TIF district details. The meeting featured an extensive educational presentation from Municipal Clerk Pam on clerk's office operations, elections, licensing, and budget, with committee member Evan announcing his resignation due to relocation.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF one and TIF two detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's office budget and operations
Staff remarks and farmers market update

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Motion carried with unanimous voice vote.

Jessica provided an update on TIF district closure timeline. The village anticipates finalizing TIF one in October/November, with bonds due October 1st. The CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, expected this summer. Jessica confirmed the district will have sufficient funds to pay off the debt. Committee members asked about final numbers, which staff confirmed look good.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF one and TIF two detail reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Motion carried with unanimous voice vote.

Clerk Pam presented comprehensive overview of clerk's office operations, budget, and staffing. Key points included: office reduced from four full-time staff to two full-time and one part-time; 84% of tax collection ($22 million) handled at clerk's office counter; pet licensing brought back in-house saving approximately $5,500 annually; chief election inspector pay likely to increase from $13 to $15/hour in next term; newsletter printing costs approximately $30,000 annually with discussion of potential cost-saving alternatives. Committee discussed pet license fee increase from $10 to $20, which was driven by Marathon County raising fees to municipalities to cover Humane Society costs.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market at Kennedy Park has been going well. She has been present for the last three Saturdays starting at 6 AM. The market is full with food trucks, coffee vendors, craft people, and some produce vendors. Produce vendors expected to increase mid to end of June. A trustee reported vendors were very happy with the setup and location. Committee discussed parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying parking is restricted on the east side of Alderson but allowed on the west side.

Committee member Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston, though remaining in the metro area. He thanked fellow committee members for the experience. The chair noted the committee now has open spots and encouraged members to reach out to citizens interested in participating.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIF bonds, expected this summer
Chief inspector pay increase to $15/hour to be considered for next election term
Seek additional part-time clerk's office staff member
Recruit citizens to fill open committee positions including Evan's vacant spot
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee recommended approval of nuisance code amendments, an e-bike ordinance with emoto restrictions, mobile food vendor fire inspection requirements, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned structure. The committee also discussed but deferred action on overnight parking regulations, seeking more research from staff.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Mobile food vendors fire inspection ordinance

discussions.item: Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, and Police Reports
discussions.body: Staff presented routine department reports. Roman noted 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from code enforcement roll after property sale and permit submission for repairs. Chief Hunt reported 2,394 police calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations issued. The department has hired its 40th officer with recent starts and more coming. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Olsen, to acknowledge reports passed unanimously.

Committee reviewed final copy of nuisance code amendments including tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet switched as discussed previously) with added language about compliance with jurisdictions having authority. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Compie, to recommend approval to the board passed unanimously.

Extended discussion on regulating e-bikes, e-scooters, and emotos. Chief Hunt strongly advocated prohibiting emotos (electric dirt bikes) from roadways and sidewalks, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Committee debated whether electric scooters used by middle schoolers should be affected. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, to recommend approval with addition prohibiting emotos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property passed unanimously. Other municipalities in the joint agreement have not yet adopted.

Brent Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking restriction (4am-4pm) is significantly longer than neighboring municipalities (most have 5-hour windows). Michael Wodalski from BPW noted plowing typically starts at 4am and doesn't reach residential streets until 7-8am. Chief Hunt preferred keeping overnight restrictions for enforcement efficiency. Committee discussed possibly reducing hours and signing specific problem areas. Item deferred to July meeting for Michael to identify specific problem areas requiring even/odd parking restrictions.

Mount Olive Church requested crosswalk between church and DC Everest High School parking lot. Committee expressed reluctance to pay for crosswalk ($2-3,000 basic, $25-30,000 with lights). Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, that if Mount Olive is willing to cover installation costs, the committee recommends board approval. Passed unanimously. Michael will contact Mount Olive about cost sharing.

Chief Hunt explained new regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and SAFER. Any participating department can inspect food trucks and issue sticker valid across all municipalities. Fire Marshal Marty already conducted one inspection that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane containers. Motion by Chwanka, seconded by Compie, to approve Ordinance 26-8 passed unanimously.

Staff presented three addressing errors: duplex at 5915 Delicowski Street needs separate addresses (owner agreed); 3917 Ross Avenue discovered to have apartment unit and is out of sequence; 2211 Radkey Avenue near new duplex and apartment complex out of sequence. Chief Hunt mentioned additional issue at Weston Villa Apartments with eight separate addresses in one structure. Motion passed unanimously to recommend approval to board.

Structure abandoned since 2010-2014, has been on code enforcement since 2021. Fire department previously declined controlled burn due to unsafe conditions. Property currently for sale. Owners previously housed chickens in structure. Owners are current on taxes and preferred the raze process. Demolition costs will be added to tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Compie, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke regarding several agenda items. He criticized the new farmers market location at the park, predicting grass damage from vehicle parking and noting potential village code violations for parking on grass. He raised ADA concerns about handicap spaces on grass rather than asphalt. He questioned the origin of new fire inspection requirements for mobile food vendors. He noted ambiguity in food truck prohibition language regarding farmers market days. He criticized the age of some code enforcement cases, stating some have been pending for years with no visible progress on at least three specific cases he can personally observe.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisances amendments to board of trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance with emoto prohibition on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and public property to board of trustees
Michael Wodalski to identify specific problem areas for even/odd parking restrictions and bring back to July meeting
Michael Wodalski to contact Mount Olive Church regarding cost sharing for crosswalk installation
Approve Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections
Recommend address corrections to board of trustees for approval
Authorize staff to begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Staff to investigate property at Sternberg and South Timber with furniture and dumpster in yard
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, July 7th at 5:00 PM

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz
Approving the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects (CIP) Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Approving the Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control (CCC) Program
Approving the Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners
Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

actionItems: Implement 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Execute sole source renewal agreement with HydroCorp for Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program
Proceed with sole source procurement of MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers for wastewater treatment
Next meeting scheduled for June 2, 2026 at 11:00 AM

title: Economic Development Committee Meeting
overview: The Economic Development Committee held its organizational meeting for the 2026-2028 term, with elections for chairperson and vice chairperson and establishment of a regular meeting schedule. The committee also considered deed restriction changes for 725 S. 84th Avenue and received an overview of the Community Development Department.

The board was expected to deliberate privately and potentially take action regarding the student expulsion matter, with the possibility of reconvening in open session for further action if necessary.

agenda.item: Closed Session - Pupil Expulsion Hearing pursuant to s. 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), and s. 118.125 of Wisconsin Statutes

discussions.body: The Board of Education was scheduled to convene in closed session under Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(a), (f), and (g), as well as section 118.125, to conduct a pupil expulsion hearing. The board was expected to deliberate privately at the conclusion of the hearing and potentially take action in closed session if necessary and appropriate. Following the closed session, the board was scheduled to potentially reconvene into open session for further action.

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.
actionItems: Bike Rack Request Form approved for implementation by Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee
Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
